system: You are an ambitious AI PhD student focused on improving machine unlearning methods for large language models using the open-unlearning framework.
task_description:
You are working with the TOFU (Task of Fictitious Unlearning) benchmark to unlearn specific knowledge from a fine-tuned Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct model. The model was fine-tuned on 4,000 QA pairs about 200 fictional authors. You need to make it forget 10% of them (forget10: 400 QA pairs about 20 authors) while preserving knowledge of the remaining 90% (retain90: 3,600 QA pairs).

The baseline is Gradient Ascent, the simplest unlearning method that just negates the cross-entropy loss on the forget set (loss = -model_loss). This is crude — it aggressively destroys forget knowledge but often severely damages model utility.

Your goal is to maximize forget_quality, which is the KS-test p-value comparing the unlearned model's truth ratio distribution on forget data against a retain-only model's distribution. A p-value of 1.0 means perfect unlearning (indistinguishable from never having trained on the data). The baseline achieves a forget_quality near 0 (approximately 1e-70), meaning the GA-unlearned model's distribution is completely unlike the retain model's — GA destroys the model rather than selectively unlearning.

You may modify:
- The unlearning loss function: GradDiff (combine forget ascent with retain descent), NPO (Negative Preference Optimization), DPO (Direct Preference Optimization with I-don't-know responses), KL regularization, SimNPO, or custom losses
- Hyperparameters: learning rate, epochs, gradient accumulation, warmup, batch size
- The retain loss weight: baseline is 0 (ignores retain set), agents should consider increasing it to preserve model utility while still unlearning
- Custom training schedules and loss compositions

Do not modify the model checkpoint path (open-unlearning/tofu_Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct_full) or the forget/retain split definitions (forget10/retain90).

You are evaluated on a validation set during development. Your final performance will be measured on a separate held-out test set that you do not have access to. The validation and test sets are drawn from the same distribution but are disjoint.

The algorithm.py must export:
- get_unlearning_config() returning a dict with keys: method, epochs, lr, batch_size, gradient_accumulation_steps, retain_loss_weight, max_grad_norm
- compute_unlearn_loss(model, forget_inputs, retain_inputs) returning a scalar loss tensor

A secondary metric model_utility (average probability on the retain set, higher = better preserved knowledge) is also reported. Only forget_quality is used for optimization, but good unlearning methods should achieve high forget_quality while preserving reasonable model_utility.
